stem: A 29-year-old primipara presents to her GP on day 7 postpartum with a 24-hour history of a painful, red right breast and fevers to 38.7 degrees Celsius. She is exclusively breastfeeding with deep nipple cracking on the right. The baby is feeding well and gaining weight appropriately. There is no fluctuance and no overlying skin breakdown beyond a 2 mm nipple fissure. She has no penicillin allergy and is otherwise well.

data_table.Vital signs: Temp 38.7 degrees Celsius, BP 118/72, HR 102, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: Right outer upper quadrant of breast erythematous, warm, tender with palpable engorgement; no fluctuant mass; nipple fissure 2 mm
data_table.Investigations: Bedside ultrasound performed: no organised collection or rim-enhancing fluid pocket; FBC and CRP not yet available

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management?

options.text: Continue breastfeeding from both breasts, ensure effective milk drainage, and start oral dicloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days
options.rationale: AU first-line for non-severe lactational mastitis is continued breastfeeding plus oral dicloxacillin or flucloxacillin, with attention to attachment and drainage. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: mastitis)

options.text: Stop breastfeeding from the affected breast immediately, suppress lactation with cabergoline, and commence oral cefalexin 500 mg six hourly
options.rationale: Stopping feeds and suppressing lactation worsens engorgement and obstructs milk drainage, which is core to resolving mastitis. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: mastitis)

options.text: Refer urgently to breast surgical clinic for incision and drainage today before any antibiotic therapy is commenced in the primary care setting
options.rationale: I&D is indicated only for an established abscess; bedside ultrasound here has excluded a collection. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: mastitis)

options.text: Trial oral paracetamol and warm compresses for 72 hours before considering antibiotic therapy or surgical referral by the GP at review
options.rationale: Symptoms beyond 24 hours with fever and a fissure warrant immediate antibiotics; delay risks abscess formation. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: mastitis)

options.text: Start oral amoxicillin-clavulanate 875/125 mg twice daily for 10 days and book a follow-up review in 2 weeks at the practice
options.rationale: Augmentin is broader than needed for non-severe mastitis; 5 days of narrow-spectrum cover is preferred to minimise infant gut and resistance impact. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: mastitis)

explanation.summary: Lactational mastitis is treated by continued effective milk removal plus narrow-spectrum oral antistaphylococcal cover (dicloxacillin or flucloxacillin) for 5 days when systemic features are present beyond 24 hours. Bedside ultrasound is useful to exclude an abscess that would need drainage. Suppressing lactation worsens stasis. Severe systemic illness, sepsis or no response by 48 hours prompts admission and IV therapy.
key_points: Continue breastfeeding through mastitis to maintain drainage
Dicloxacillin or flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 5 days is first-line
Persistent symptoms beyond 48 hours of antibiotics warrant ultrasound for abscess
explanation.pearls: Mastitis improves within 48 hours of effective drainage plus dicloxacillin; failure to improve should trigger breast ultrasound to exclude an organised abscess.

stem: A 27-year-old G1P0 presents to ED at 9+4 weeks gestation with 5 days of intractable nausea and vomiting 10 to 12 times daily, unable to tolerate oral fluids for 24 hours. She has lost 3.5 kg since her booking 2 weeks ago. She is otherwise well, with no abdominal pain, vaginal bleeding or features of pre-existing eating disorder. Her dating ultrasound at 7 weeks showed a singleton intrauterine pregnancy with cardiac activity. She has tried oral pyridoxine without benefit.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 102/64, HR 112, RR 16, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.6 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Dry mucosae, sunken eyes, capillary refill 3 seconds, abdomen soft and non-tender
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+, urine specific gravity 1.030, Na 132 mmol/L, K 3.2 mmol/L, urea 8.1 mmol/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L, TSH 0.4 mU/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Discharge home with oral metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily and ginger capsules with planned GP review in 48 hours
options.rationale: Severe hyperemesis with 3+ ketonuria and hypokalaemia mandates admission and IV therapy, not outpatient oral antiemetics. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

options.text: Admit, give intravenous thiamine 100 mg before commencing intravenous fluids with normal saline plus potassium replacement and ondansetron 4 mg IV
options.rationale: Thiamine prophylaxis before glucose-containing fluids prevents Wernicke encephalopathy in prolonged hyperemesis; IV rehydration with potassium replacement is first-line. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

options.text: Commence intravenous 5 percent dextrose at 125 mL/hour without prior thiamine to address hypoglycaemia and ketosis quickly in the department
options.rationale: Giving dextrose before thiamine in a prolonged starvation state can precipitate Wernicke encephalopathy; thiamine must come first. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

options.text: Commence oral prednisolone 40 mg daily as an outpatient with weekly clinical review for 2 weeks in the obstetric day assessment unit
options.rationale: Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis after multiple antiemetics have failed, not as first-line outpatient therapy. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

options.text: Send her home with subcutaneous ondansetron 8 mg pumps and arrange community midwife daily review and outpatient bloods at the local pathology
options.rationale: Severe dehydration with electrolyte derangement is not safely managed in the community; admission is required. (Source: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline)
source_refs: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum with significant ketonuria, dehydration and electrolyte derangement requires admission for IV rehydration. Thiamine 100 mg IV is given before glucose-containing fluids to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy. Stepwise antiemetics include pyridoxine plus doxylamine, metoclopramide, ondansetron and second-line therapies such as steroids if refractory. Ondansetron is regarded as safe from the first trimester onwards.
key_points: Thiamine 100 mg IV precedes glucose-containing fluids in prolonged vomiting
Ondansetron is now considered safe from the first trimester for hyperemesis
Corticosteroids are reserved for refractory hyperemesis after stepwise failure
explanation.pearls: Always give thiamine 100 mg IV before dextrose-containing fluids in any patient with more than 5 days of poor oral intake, including hyperemesis gravidarum, to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.

sources.label: RANZCOG Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy guideline

stem: A 28-year-old G2P0 presents to the early pregnancy assessment service with 4 days of mild right iliac fossa discomfort and small amounts of dark vaginal spotting. Her last menstrual period was 6 weeks and 2 days ago. She is haemodynamically stable, has no peritonism and no fertility concerns about future pregnancies. She has no liver, renal or haematological disease and is happy to attend twice-weekly follow-up. She is not breastfeeding and is using no medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/72, HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 36.7 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Mild right iliac fossa tenderness, no guarding, no rebound; cervical os closed
data_table.Investigations: Serum beta-hCG 2400 IU/L; TVS shows an empty uterus, a 28 mm right tubal mass without fetal cardiac activity and no free fluid in the pouch of Douglas; LFTs and FBC normal; creatinine 64 micromol/L

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate management option?

options.text: Expectant management with weekly serum beta-hCG until the level falls below 5 IU/L and no further imaging required during follow-up
options.rationale: Expectant care is reserved for hCG below about 1000 with falling trend; at 2400 with a visible adnexal mass the spontaneous resolution rate is too low. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Urgent laparoscopic salpingectomy under general anaesthesia as the safest definitive treatment regardless of the patient's clinical stability today
options.rationale: Surgery is indicated for haemodynamic instability, rupture, contraindication to methotrexate or failed medical therapy; this patient meets none of those. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Single-dose intramuscular methotrexate 50 mg/m2 with day 4 and day 7 serum beta-hCG to assess response and rule out rising titres
options.rationale: She meets AU criteria for medical management: hCG below 5000, no FHB, mass under 35 mm, stable, no peritonism, normal LFT/FBC/creatinine and reliable follow-up. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Diagnostic uterine evacuation and curettage to confirm absence of intrauterine villi before deciding on any further treatment route
options.rationale: When TVS already shows an adnexal mass with empty uterus and hCG above the discriminatory zone, dilatation and curettage adds no information and delays treatment. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

options.text: Repeat the serum beta-hCG and ultrasound in 48 hours, with no specific treatment initiated today regardless of the current imaging findings
options.rationale: A non-tubal pregnancy of unknown location requires repeat assessment; here a definitive adnexal ectopic is already demonstrated, mandating treatment now. (Source: RANZCOG ectopic pregnancy guideline)

explanation.summary: Medical management of ectopic pregnancy with single-dose methotrexate is appropriate when hCG is below 5000, the ectopic mass is under 35 mm without fetal cardiac activity, the woman is stable with no peritonism, follow-up is reliable, and there is no contraindication on LFT, FBC or renal function. Day 4 and day 7 hCG are used to assess success: a >= 15 percent fall between day 4 and 7 predicts resolution. Surgical management is reserved for instability, rupture or methotrexate failure.
key_points: Methotrexate eligibility: hCG below 5000, no FHB, ectopic mass under 35 mm, stable patient, normal LFT/FBC/renal
Day 4 to day 7 hCG drop of >= 15 percent confirms response, otherwise consider repeat dose or surgery
Surgical salpingectomy or salpingostomy is for instability, rupture, contraindications or failed medical therapy
explanation.pearls: After single-dose methotrexate the hCG often rises between day 1 and 4; the meaningful drop is between day 4 and day 7, and a fall of less than 15 percent triggers a second dose or surgery.

sources.label: AMH: methotrexate dosing in ectopic pregnancy

subtopic_detail: VBAC suitability after one previous lower segment caesarean
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 attends her 36-week appointment to discuss mode of birth after one previous lower segment caesarean section at term for breech presentation 2 years ago. The current pregnancy is uncomplicated with a cephalic singleton, estimated fetal weight on the 60th centile. She has no diabetes, no hypertension, no prior uterine rupture and a uterine scar interval over 18 months. She is well informed and would prefer to avoid repeat caesarean if her risk profile permits.

data_table.Examination: Symphyseal-fundal height appropriate at 35 cm; fetus cephalic; cervix unfavourable for ripening yet
data_table.Investigations: Booking bloods normal; placenta fundal anterior on 20-week morphology and 32-week growth scan; uterine artery Doppler indices normal

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate counselling and plan?

options.text: Counsel that elective repeat caesarean at 39 weeks is mandatory because trial of labour is contraindicated after any prior caesarean delivery in Australia
options.rationale: VBAC is broadly supported after one prior lower segment scar with cephalic singleton; routine elective caesarean is not mandated. (Source: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement

options.text: Recommend induction of labour with prostaglandin gel at 39 weeks regardless of cervical favourability to optimise the spontaneous labour window
options.rationale: Prostaglandins double uterine rupture risk in scarred uteri and are generally avoided; mechanical or oxytocin-based methods are preferred when induction is needed. (Source: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement

options.text: Plan home birth with a private midwife given the otherwise low-risk pregnancy and her strong preference to avoid hospital intervention this time
options.rationale: Trial of labour after caesarean requires intrapartum CTG and ready access to theatre for category 1 caesarean; home birth is not appropriate. (Source: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement

options.text: Offer a planned trial of labour after caesarean in a hospital with continuous CTG and immediate theatre access, with elective repeat caesarean from 39 weeks as an alternative
options.rationale: She meets criteria for VBAC: one prior LSCS with non-recurrent indication, cephalic singleton, no contraindication; hospital-based monitoring with theatre access is standard. (Source: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement)
source_refs: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement

options.text: Offer external cephalic version now to confirm presentation and book elective caesarean if the fetus reverts to a non-cephalic lie at 38 weeks
options.rationale: The fetus is already cephalic; ECV is not indicated and the question is mode of birth, not presentation correction. (Source: RANZCOG external cephalic version statement)

explanation.summary: Planned VBAC is supported after one prior lower segment caesarean for a non-recurrent indication, in a cephalic singleton pregnancy, in a hospital with continuous CTG and immediate theatre access. Quoted risks are uterine rupture about 0.5 percent and overall VBAC success of 60 to 80 percent. Prostaglandins are avoided for induction in scarred uteri because they roughly double rupture risk. Counselling must be balanced, documented, and offered alongside elective repeat caesarean.
key_points: VBAC is offered after one LSCS with non-recurrent indication and a cephalic singleton
Uterine rupture risk is around 0.5 percent during planned VBAC
Prostaglandin induction in a scarred uterus roughly doubles rupture risk and is usually avoided
explanation.pearls: Planned VBAC success after one prior LSCS for a non-recurrent indication is around 70 percent, with uterine rupture about 1 in 200, in an Australian tertiary unit with continuous CTG.

sources.label: RANZCOG birth after previous caesarean statement

stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to ED with 4 days of watery non-bloody diarrhoea 6 times daily after a 7-day course of oral amoxicillin-clavulanate for cellulitis ended 10 days ago. She is haemodynamically stable, mildly dehydrated, and tolerating oral fluids. She has no abdominal distension, no peritonism and no ileus. She takes a proton pump inhibitor for reflux and lives independently with her husband at home.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/76, HR 92, RR 16, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 37.4 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Mild lower abdominal tenderness without guarding; bowel sounds present; mucous membranes a little dry
data_table.Investigations: WCC 11.4 x10^9/L, creatinine 78 micromol/L (baseline 70), lactate 1.4 mmol/L, CRP 32 mg/L, stool PCR positive for Clostridioides difficile toxin B gene

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate antimicrobial choice?

options.text: Intravenous metronidazole 500 mg eight hourly for 10 days alongside ongoing oral PPI therapy to suppress gastric acid recurrence
options.rationale: IV metronidazole achieves poor colonic concentrations in CDI; current AU and international guidance favours oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin even for non-severe disease. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: C difficile)

options.text: Oral loperamide 4 mg followed by 2 mg after each loose stool and no antibiotic therapy initiated until repeat testing in 48 hours
options.rationale: Antimotility agents are contraindicated in CDI because of toxic megacolon risk; treatment should not be delayed in a PCR-positive symptomatic case. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: C difficile)

options.text: Oral rifaximin 400 mg three times daily for 14 days as the first-line agent for community-acquired Clostridioides difficile infection in adults
options.rationale: Rifaximin is used as a tapered chaser for recurrent CDI in some protocols, not as primary therapy for a first episode. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: C difficile)

options.text: Faecal microbiota transplant via colonoscopy as the first-line approach for an initial non-severe Clostridioides difficile episode in this patient
options.rationale: FMT is reserved for multiply recurrent CDI after standard antibiotic regimens have failed; it is not first-line for an initial episode. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: C difficile)

options.text: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days with cessation of the inciting antibiotic and ongoing oral rehydration support
options.rationale: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days is the AU first-line regimen for non-severe CDI; metronidazole is no longer preferred. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: C difficile)

explanation.summary: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days has become first-line for non-severe Clostridioides difficile infection in Australia, displacing metronidazole on the strength of higher cure and reduced recurrence. Fidaxomicin is an alternative, particularly when recurrence risk is high. Severe disease, ileus, megacolon or sepsis prompts admission, oral plus rectal vancomycin and surgical review. Antimotility agents are avoided. FMT is reserved for multiple recurrences.
key_points: Oral vancomycin 125 mg QID for 10 days is first-line in non-severe CDI
Loperamide is contraindicated because of toxic megacolon risk
FMT is reserved for multiply recurrent disease after standard antibiotic failure
explanation.pearls: Stop the inciting antibiotic whenever possible at CDI diagnosis; this alone resolves around a quarter of mild cases before oral vancomycin has finished a 10-day course.

sources.label: ACSQHC: Clostridioides difficile clinical care standard
